era_context: The mid-14th century continued to be marked by the Hundred Years' War, Ottoman expansion, the final decades of Yuan Dynasty rule in China, and the ongoing demographic and social recovery from the Black Death. The Papacy remained in Avignon. European feudalism was under strain from labour shortages and peasant unrest.

events.title: Jacquerie peasant uprising in France
events.description: The Jacquerie was a major peasant uprising that occurred in northern France in May–June 1358, triggered by the devastation of the Hundred Years' War, heavy taxation, and the nobility's perceived failure to protect the peasantry. Peasants (jacquerie from 'Jacques,' a common name) rose up against the nobility, burning castles and killing nobles. The uprising was brutally suppressed by French and English nobility within weeks, with thousands of peasants killed. The Jacquerie is significant as one of the earliest large-scale peasant revolts in medieval Europe and as evidence of the social strain caused by prolonged warfare and plague aftermath.
key_figures: Charles V (King of France, then Dauphin)
Jean de Grailly (Captal de Buch, English commander)
events.certainty_note: The Jacquerie is well-documented in contemporary and near-contemporary French and English chronicles. The dating to May–June 1358 is secure.
cross_references: 1381-001 (Peasants' Revolt in England, similar social dynamics)

events.title: Peace negotiations between England and France intensify
events.description: Following the Jacquerie and amid mutual exhaustion from the Hundred Years' War, England and France intensified peace negotiations in 1358. These negotiations would culminate in the Treaty of Brétigny (1360). The year 1358 saw diplomatic missions and preliminary agreements that set the framework for the formal treaty. Both kingdoms were weakened by plague, warfare, and internal unrest, making peace negotiations more attractive than continued conflict.
events.certainty_note: Diplomatic records confirm intensive peace negotiations in 1358.

events.title: Zhu Yuanzhang's military expansion continues
events.description: Zhu Yuanzhang continued his military campaigns against rival warlords and Yuan forces in 1358. His control of the Yangtze River region was expanding, and he was consolidating his administrative apparatus. The capture of key cities and the recruitment of talented generals and administrators strengthened his position as the leading force in southern China. By 1358, it was becoming clear that Zhu Yuanzhang would be the one to unify China and overthrow the Yuan Dynasty.
events.certainty_note: Zhu Yuanzhang's military campaigns are well-documented in both Ming and Yuan sources.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans
events.description: The Ottoman Empire continued its expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans during 1358. Under the leadership of Murad I (though his formal accession is dated 1362, military campaigns under his direction began earlier), Ottoman forces were consolidating control over Thrace and threatening Byzantine and Serbian territories. The Ottoman expansion during this period was facilitated by the weakness of the Byzantine Empire and the fragmentation of Serbian power after the death of Stefan Dušan (1355).
key_figures: Murad I (Ottoman military leader, future Sultan)
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in this period is attested in Byzantine sources, but the precise dating of campaigns in 1358 is uncertain due to reliance on later Ottoman compilations.

events.title: Innocent VI's papacy and ecclesiastical reform efforts
events.description: Pope Innocent VI (r. 1352–1362) continued his efforts to reform the Avignon papacy and address ecclesiastical corruption. In 1358, the papacy was facing increasing pressure from reformers and from secular rulers to return to Rome. Innocent VI's papacy was marked by attempts to reduce nepotism and to strengthen papal authority in the face of growing national churches. The period 1358–1362 saw the papacy grappling with the question of its temporal and spiritual authority.
key_figures: Petrarch (humanist and critic of the Avignon papacy)
events.certainty_note: Innocent VI's papacy and reform efforts are well-documented in papal records and contemporary correspondence.

disconfirming_evidence: The Jacquerie is sometimes portrayed as a proto-revolutionary movement with clear ideological goals, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it was primarily a response to immediate grievances (war, taxation, noble violence) rather than a coherent political program. The uprising was also more localized and less unified than some accounts suggest. Additionally, the connection between the Jacquerie and later peasant movements (such as the English Peasants' Revolt of 1381) is often overstated; the two movements had different causes and contexts, though both reflected underlying social strain.
historiographic_note: The documentation for 1358 is again heavily weighted toward Western Europe, particularly France and England, due to the survival of royal and ecclesiastical records. The Jacquerie is well-documented because it was a major event that affected the nobility and was recorded in chronicles. Chinese sources are abundant but often compiled retrospectively. Ottoman sources are later compilations. The perspectives of the peasants themselves in the Jacquerie are largely absent from the historical record; we know about the uprising primarily through the accounts of nobles and clergy who opposed it.

graph_edges.note: The Jacquerie in France (1358) and the Peasants' Revolt in England (1381) were separated by 23 years but reflected similar social dynamics of peasant unrest in the aftermath of the Black Death
Peace negotiations in 1358 led to the Treaty of Brétigny in 1360
Zhu Yuanzhang's military expansion in 1358 contributed to the establishment of the Ming Dynasty in 1368
Ottoman expansion in 1358 continued under Murad I's formal accession in 1362
